item.content:encoded: The wording of the last footnote just hit a nerve for me, saving the age for last.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like to ask you what things make it too much of a resemblance to SCP-1762? Because personally, the only resemblance I see is the theme about sentient origamis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this one just bears too much resemblance to “Where the dragons went”, especially that last part with the note. It honestly just comes off as a knockoff of that one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot here. I apologise if some of it is unclear; I'm pretty tired right now but if I didn't do it now I'd just forget.

Lots of notes
– hide block

fresh supply of square papers

most of its time searching for papers

Only papers below 50 g/m²

In each of these cases it should be "paper", no "s".

square papers with a dimension of 15 x 15 cm

This would be cleaner as "with dimensions of 15 x 15 cm", but you could also just trim it right down and say "paper measuring 15 x 15 cm".

Only papers below 50 g/m² can be given to SCP-4393 to slow down the rate of degradation.

Adding a comma after "SCP-4393" will make this easier to read.

may cause unforeseen irreversible damages.

Should be "damage", no "s".

SCP-4393 is a sentient, origami crane

No comma is necessary here.

SCP-4393 uses it two, relatively manipulative

Remove the comma here.

and will be collectively referred to as SCP-4393-1 instances.

You can remove the word "instances" here. It's not necessarily wrong, but it's just a little redundant and the sentence reads better without it.

Destroying an SCP-4393-1 instance or when SCP-4393 is handled by any personnel triggers an aggressive behavior

This whole fragment here is a little clunky to read. It would be better phrased as something like "Directly handling SCP-4393 or destroying an instance of SCP-4939-1 triggers an aggressive response[…]"
This also removes the word "behaviour", which is used again later in the sentence. Repeated words like this can sometimes make a sentence read fairly awkwardly, so it helps the whole thing flow a bit better without it.

towards the personnel responsible for any of the conditions.

You can remove the "for any of the conditions" part here. It's a little self-reinforcing, which is unnecessary since it's still part of the same sentence describing the situation in the first place.

However, due to their lack of substantial physical strength

No need for the word "substantial" here. It's an unnecessary qualifier as it implies that substantial strength is required to be a threat (which clearly isn't the case as these things become very dangerous later on in the article.)

Most of the contained SCP-4393-1 instances will continuously collide itself with the containment walls, possibly as an attempt to escape. The instance will repeat this behavior until it becomes immobilized due to crumpling.

There are a few issues with these 2 sentences. The "Most of the" part makes the whole thing feel unclinical, use of the word "itself" in "continuously collide itself with" is incorrect as the sentence already has a subject ("the contained SCP-4393-1 instances"), and then in the second sentence you begin to refer to them by the singular.
Consider rewording to something like "Contained SCP-4393-1 instances will continually collide with the walls of their containment cell, possibly in an attempt to escape. Instances will repeat this behaviour until they are immobilized by the damage caused."

If SCP-4393 is prevented from creating any SCP-4393-1 instances, the entity will display self-destructive behavior such as repeatedly flying into walls and personnel.

You don't need the word "any" in here as it adds an odd condition to the statement. You can also just say "it" instead of "the entity", as it is already the subject of the sentence.
Additionally, and this isn't wrong but just a thought, since you already described this behaviour for the -1 instances, you could just refer back to it here. For example, "If SCP-4393 is prevented from creating SCP-4939-1 instances, it will display self-destructive behaviour similar to that of previously created instances in containment."

Restricting its movement via trapping it between a heavy object or attaching it to an adhesive surface also triggers the destructive behavior, resulting in tears and further degradation particularly at its creases.

I don't think you need all of these examples in here, they just kind of bloat out the sentence. You can just say "Restricting its movement also triggers destructive behaviour, resulting in tears[…]"

Possibly due to substantial degradation, SCP-4393 ceased movement on 23/5/1958 at exactly 19:55 and was confirmed to be neutralized. Prior to this, SCP-4393 was halfway done folding another SCP-4393-1 instance.

Beginning the sentence with speculation rather than the actual subject of the sentence makes it feel indirect and slow-paced. The second sentence here also reads very unclinically (phrases like "halfway done" aren't clinical.) Consider moving "possibly due to substantial degradation" from the start of the sentence to the end, and replace "halfway done" with something like "in the process of".

After seven seconds had passed, all instances were observed to begin shaking

Unless it specifically being seven seconds is important, I'd just say "Several seconds later", otherwise you might give the impression that it specifically being seven seconds has some significance. Additionally, they actually did begin shaking, so you don't need the "were observed to" qualifier. It actually happened, so you can just say "all instances began shaking".

finished the uncompleted origami crane which immediately gained sentience.

Add a comma between "crane" and "which".

All the SCP-4393-1 instances began

You can remove the word "all" here. It's already clear you are referring to all of them.

The aggregate momentarily collapsed in on itself creating a loud shockwave, composed of multiple crane calls which instantaneously caused all paper

You've got the comma in the wrong place here; move it from after "shockwave" to after "calls".

began to move in a westerly direction from the site and accelerate at an unprecedented speed of 1200 kph

This reads a little incredulously, which isn't something you want in a clinical document. Additionally, "accelerate" should be "accelerating", but I don't think you mean acceleration here, I think you just mean "moving". ie "began to move in a westerly direction at a speed of 1200kp/h".

SCP-4393-1 instances began mobbing on the aircraft

Remove the word "on" here.

resulting in the destruction of three of the aircraft due to engine failure and severely damaging the other two which were ordered to retreat.

Due to the repeated use of the word "aircraft" here this reads a little oddly. Consider rewording it to something like "SCP-4393-1 instances began mobbing the aircraft, destroying three of them and severely damaging the other two, which were ordered to retreat."

witnesses were amnesticed

Generally "amnesticised" is used in this case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see.
Can I ask you to point them out so I can try fixing them? (Grammar has always been a huge trouble for me.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is nice and tells a decent story, but I feel like this could have used another editing pass. You have a lot of weird grammar and word choice issues throughout most of the article that somewhat hampers my enjoyment of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fixed the xxxx that was left in

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This started as arsonist cranes if I remember right? Cool what became of that base.

I think that might've been someone else.

Glad you like it though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really sweet. A bit bittersweet seing how many got destroyed and how ultimately they did not actually do anything but still heart warming overall. Got me emotional when I did not even know the original story. I really liked the imagery of a giant cluster of these letting ot an actual crane call.
This started as arsonist paper cranes if I remember right? Cool what became of that base.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Big thanks to Humanmale who helped me a lot in polishing and developing the initial draft of this, and to the wonderful Butterly Squad Critters, Shanor and Laneous, for further polishing and filling in some minor but necessary to be filled plotholes, respectively. Thanks to Schumacher for also checking out the draft.

Edit: Another big thanks to Mortos for his/her help.

The first image is a cropped image from here, https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Origami-cranes-tobefree-20151223-222820.jpg

The second image is from here, https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Starling_murmuration.jpg
